title: Play in Adulthood
subtitle: A reading on why adults stop playing and what it costs them
excerpt: A reading on the role of play in adult life. Looks at what play is, why adults largely stop doing it, what the research says about its function, and what genuine adult play looks like when it is not disguised as exercise or productivity.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on the research of Stuart Brown and research into adult play and creative activity. Stuart Brown and Christopher Vaughan, Play: How It Shapes the Brain, Opens the Imagination, and Invigorates the Soul (2009), Avery. Primary source for Brown's play taxonomy and adult play research.

content.text: A reading on play in adult life. Most adults stopped playing at some point and have a complicated relationship with the idea of starting again. This reading looks at what play actually is, why it matters, and what it looks like in an adult life when it isn't pretending to be something more productive.

Play is activity done for its own sake. There is no goal beyond the activity itself. Researcher Stuart Brown has spent decades studying play across species. He identifies several key features. Play is voluntary. It has no obvious biological purpose beyond the activity. It involves a shift in subjective time. The desire to continue comes from inside the activity, not from an external reward.

This definition excludes most of what adults call leisure. Exercise done to manage weight is not play. Hobbies tracked in productivity apps are not play. Social events managed as networking are not play. These are fine things, but they are not play in the sense that matters. Play has no agenda.

Adults stop playing through a combination of social pressure and a lack of structural opportunity. Children play because they have time and because it is expected of them. Adults face neither condition. Time is the scarce resource in adult life, and the cultural expectation in most professional environments is that time not spent productively requires justification.

There is also a self-consciousness dimension. Play requires a willingness to look foolish, to be uncertain about the outcome, to do something you're not good at. Adults, who have spent years building competence in specific domains, often find this uncomfortable. The beginner's position, which play frequently requires, sits uneasily alongside a professional identity.

Play in adults is associated with creativity, problem-solving flexibility, emotional regulation, and relationship quality. Brown's research found that adults who had no play in their lives showed more rigidity in thinking, more difficulty with change, and lower wellbeing than those who maintained some form of play. The play does not need to be large. It needs to be genuine: done for its own sake, without an outcome agenda.

Adult play varies significantly by person. For some people it is physical: sport played not to win but because of the sensation of playing. For others it is creative: making things for the satisfaction of the making. For others it is social: a specific kind of conversation with specific people where the interaction itself is the point. For others it is imaginative: reading fiction, playing games, building things with no particular purpose.

The test for whether something is play is the absence of outcome pressure. If the activity would be abandoned the moment it became useful or productive or measurable, it is probably play. If it would be abandoned the moment it stopped being useful or productive or measurable, it probably isn't.
Written for homemade.education. The reading draws on Stuart Brown's research into the neuroscience and psychology of play across the lifespan. Full citation in source notes.
